g.data-narration: Welcome! We explore the fundamental trade-off between memory speed, size, and cost that shapes modern computer architecture.
g.text: Speed, Size and Cost of Memory Systems

g.data-narration: Computers need memory that is fast, large, and cheap. No single technology achieves all three, creating a fundamental design challenge.
g.text: A computer system requires memory that is simultaneously:

g.data-narration: This table shows how memory technologies trade speed for size and cost. Faster memory costs more per bit and comes in smaller capacities.
SRAM uses flip-flops (6 transistors per bit) with no refresh needed, while DRAM uses 1 transistor + 1 capacitor requiring periodic refresh.
The memory hierarchy arranges memory levels from fastest/smallest (registers) to slowest/largest (secondary storage) to optimize performance.
Each hierarchy level has distinct characteristics: registers are under 1ns, cache is 1-20ns, DRAM is 50-100ns, and storage is microseconds to milliseconds.
Programs exhibit locality: recently accessed data is likely accessed again (temporal), and nearby data will be accessed soon (spatial).
g.text: The memory hierarchy works because programs exhibit:
Recently accessed items likely accessed again

g.data-narration: Cache memory stores frequently accessed data. A hit means data found in cache (fast); a miss requires fetching from slower main memory.
g.text: Not in cache - fetch from RAM

g.data-narration: Average Memory Access Time (AMAT) combines cache and memory access times weighted by hit rate to measure effective memory speed.
The average cost per bit of a hierarchical system is dominated by cheap large memory, while performance is dominated by fast cache memory.
In summary: faster memory costs more per bit and is smaller; SRAM is for cache, DRAM for main memory; hierarchy with locality achieves 90-98% hit rates.

g.text: Speed, size, cost are inversely related
SRAM for cache, DRAM for main memory
